statement: The rewrite should introduce a provider capability registry and provider-specific extension points, so that the core engine consumes normalized capabilities and quota dimensions instead of baking OpenAI-shaped assumptions into workflows, ledgers, and rate-limit code.
rationale: Official docs show meaningful divergence among OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini in quota dimensions, batch semantics, caching, and tool/structured-output behavior. A provider-agnostic workflow runtime only remains maintainable if those differences are modeled explicitly rather than hidden in ad hoc conditionals.
keyAssumptions: Judge-gym intends to support at least OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini in the rewritten engine
The team prefers one shared execution engine with adapter boundaries over fully separate provider-specific pipelines
validationCriteria: Adding a new provider does not require changing core workflow or idempotency code for basic sync support, and optional capabilities like batch remain registry-driven rather than hard-coded
Rate-limit key generation derives from provider capability metadata rather than OpenAI-specific metric names
The attempt ledger can store provider-specific metadata without forcing the top-level schema to mirror one provider's wire format
critiquePoints: An overly abstract capability registry can become vague and underpowered if it is not grounded in real provider differences
Some provider features may still justify provider-specific flows rather than forcing everything through a generic operation type
questions: Which capability fields belong in shared contracts versus runtime-only adapter code?
Should batchability be declared at the model level, provider level, or both?
